<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision.qmd@c1024905a
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision

The stack needs a memory for what it believes.

That memory cannot be ordinary prose. Prose is too fluid. It can soften a
claim, sharpen it, repeat it, translate it into a diagram, summarize it in a
release note, or polish it for a reader without preserving the exact reason
the claim was allowed to sound that way. If the evidence changes, the prose
may change too, but nothing about a paragraph guarantees that the old state is
still visible.

A claim ledger gives belief an address. It says: this claim has an identity;
this is its scope; this is what supports it; this is what challenges it; this
is where it appears; this is how it changed. The ledger does not make the
claim true. It makes the system accountable for having said it.

That distinction matters for any living book, and it matters even more for a
system that plans, writes, verifies, routes, publishes, and improves itself.
Without a ledger, confidence can drift. A cautious sentence becomes a cleaner
summary. The summary becomes a heading. The heading becomes a release claim.
Nobody intended to promote the claim, but the reader meets a stronger version
than the evidence earned. The claim ledger exists to stop that quiet promotion.

Belief revision is the other half of the same discipline. A serious system
must be able to change its mind without pretending it was always right. It must
be able to downgrade a claim, split it into narrower claims, merge equivalent
variants, preserve contradictions, route uncertainty to review, or retire a
claim whose support has failed. Those are not editorial embarrassments. They
are maintenance operations on the system's epistemic state.

The local contribution here is continuity. Evidence States explains when a
claim may move; the claim ledger keeps the claim recognizable while wording,
surface, source context, contradiction state, and revision history change. It
protects the old trail so the system can improve its belief without erasing
how it got there.

## Problem

Verification bandwidth decides whether a context packet is adequate for a
claim. Claim ledgers decide what happens after a claim enters the architecture.

Once a system has admitted context, generated prose, run a proof check, cited a
source, or reviewed a result, it still needs to remember what the claim is. The
same statement may appear in a chapter, appendix, diagram, benchmark report,
reader edition, audio script, issue, release note, or runtime artifact. If
those surfaces are not tied to one claim identity, the stack cannot tell
whether it is repeating one supported claim, producing a narrower variant, or
accidentally contradicting itself.

The ledger also has to remember absence. A claim can be unsupported, supported
only by a source that has not been passage-reviewed, supported by stale
evidence, contradicted by a later result, blocked by a missing proof, or
waiting for human review. Those states should not collapse into vague
uncertainty. They ask for different next actions.

A living book makes the problem visible. Appendix C is the book's public claim
ledger: every chapter core claim has a support state, source mapping, and
promotion path. That is useful for the book, but the deeper architectural point
is broader. Any self-improving system needs claim identity, evidence state,
contradiction links, uncertainty, revision history, and publication boundaries
as first-class objects.

This is where adequacy becomes memory. The previous chapter can say that a
claim was not verified strongly enough. The ledger keeps that result from
evaporating when the claim is restated, summarized, diagrammed, or rewritten
for a reader.

## Why Ordinary Prose Fails

A model can write a persuasive answer without maintaining the evidence state
behind each sentence.

That is not a flaw in style. It is a mismatch between language and belief
management. Language is optimized for communication. Belief management needs
identity, provenance, state, change history, and refusal conditions. A
paragraph can imply all of those things, but implication is too weak for a
governed architecture.

The failure has several forms.

First, assumptions remain untracked. A system says something because it was
useful for the argument, but no record asks whether that sentence is a claim,
a hypothesis, an example, an analogy, a result, or a source-derived fact.

Second, contradictions disappear during revision. The next draft reads
smoothly, but the reason for the change is gone. The reader sees a cleaner
belief state, not the evidence that forced the change.

Third, repeated surfaces launder confidence. A claim that was careful in the
main chapter becomes stronger in a summary, simpler in a diagram, and more
definite in a release note. Nothing was formally promoted, but the language
became more confident anyway.

External systems point at pieces of the same pressure. ALCE asks whether
generated answers are supported by citations. Self-RAG connects retrieval and
critique to generation. CheckList turns behavioral expectations into test
matrices. Those are useful comparators, not proof that this stack implements a
complete belief engine. The ASI Stack uses the same underlying lesson: claims
need records, tests, and refusal paths, not just fluent wording.

There is also an older lineage underneath the word "belief." AGM belief
revision treats contraction and revision as formal operations on theories.
Truth-maintenance systems preserve the reasons behind program beliefs.
Assumption-based truth maintenance tracks which conclusions depend on which
assumptions, including across inconsistent contexts. The claim ledger is not a
new name for those systems. It borrows their discipline and aims it at the
surfaces a living AI project actually publishes: chapters, diagrams, release
notes, evidence records, reader editions, and future artifacts.

The ledger therefore protects two directions at once. It prevents weak claims
from becoming stronger through repetition, and it lets strong claims become
weaker when evidence fails without making the correction disappear.

## Core Claim

Claim ledgers should maintain support states, provenance, contradiction links,
uncertainty, and revision history for material claims. In the live book, this
chapter's core claim remains a design rationale at `argument` support.

Spinoza supplies the strongest reasoning source for the claim-ledger layer: claims
should be proposed separately from verification, represented with provenance
and tiers, revised when contradictions appear, and downgraded when proof,
citation, or procedure support fails. VIEA supplies the execution pressure:
important outputs become durable artifacts, claims receive support states, and
failures become residuals. Aletheia and UAT supply claim-native artifacts,
verification gates, adversarial review, dossier-bounded proposition states,
and human adjudication paths. Coherence Exchange remains connector/source-note
governance vocabulary, not external corroboration.

The current repository supports only a bounded part of this idea. It has
public claim and belief-revision record schemas, fixture validation, a
synthetic claim-ledger revision harness, and finite Lean predicates over
claim-update and belief-revision records. Those artifacts support record
discipline. They do not prove open-domain claim extraction, contradiction
detection, source accuracy, semantic equivalence, citation correctness,
verifier quality, substantive belief revision, or deployed epistemic
correctness.

## Mechanism

A claim ledger turns belief into a durable object.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Surface["Prose, diagram, report, or release note"] --> Extract["Candidate claim"]
  Extract --> Record["Claim record"]
  Record --> Evidence["Evidence refs and support state"]
  Record --> Scope["Scope and surface refs"]
  Evidence --> Scan["Contradictions, defeaters, and residuals"]
  Scope --> Scan
  Scan --> Decision{"Revision needed?"}
  Decision -- "no" --> Retain["Retain current state"]
  Decision -- "yes" --> Revise["Downgrade, split, merge, block, or retire"]
  Retain --> History["Revision history"]
  Revise --> History
  History --> Ledger["Claim ledger"]
  Ledger --> Boundary["Publication support boundary"]
```

The ledger begins with a claim record. A useful record names the claim ID,
claim text or text variants, scope, source refs, surface refs, support state,
claim label, uncertainty, evidence refs, contradiction refs, defeaters,
review state, lifecycle state, and non-claims.

The revision record then names the transition. It records the prior support
state, new support state, changed surfaces, contradiction state, residuals,
revision reason, history refs, promotion blockers, non-overwrite attestation,
ledger effect, and non-claims. The point is not to make every revision
expensive. The point is to keep important belief changes from becoming
invisible.

The lifecycle should be explicit. Claims can be proposed, recorded, mapped,
challenged, revised, downgraded, deprecated, split, merged, promoted, retired,
or blocked. Promotion is only one state change. A downgrade can be more
valuable than a promotion when it prevents the architecture from spending false
confidence.

This mechanism makes prose a view over the ledger. A chapter can be beautiful,
a diagram can be compact, and a reader edition can be humane, but none of
those surfaces should silently change a claim's support state. If the meaning,
scope, or confidence changes, the ledger changes too.

## Interfaces

The claim ledger sits between context, verification, publication, and review.

VCM and verification-bandwidth records tell the system what context was
admitted and what it can support. Spinoza receives selected high-value claims
when proof-, citation-, or procedure-carrying support is needed. UAT and
Aletheia-style review paths handle claims that are contested, inferred,
unsupported, dossier-bounded, or high stakes. Appendix C records the book-level
support boundary so the public manuscript cannot pretend a claim has been
promoted when it has not.

The most important interface is the Belief Revision Record. At minimum, it
should name:

- `revision_id`
- `claim_id`
- `claim_scope`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `prior_support_state`
- `new_support_state`
- `evidence_refs`
- `surface_refs`
- `contradiction_refs`
- `contradiction_state`
- `defeaters`
- `residual_refs`
- `revision_reason`
- `uncertainty`
- `review_status`
- `history_refs`
- `changed_surface_refs`
- `non_overwrite_attestation`
- `ledger_effect`
- `promotion_blockers`
- `non_claims`

Those fields do not extract claims or detect contradictions by themselves.
They make later extraction, review, editing, and release work accountable to a
stable belief object.

## Invariants

Claims must be separable from prose. If a claim cannot be addressed outside
the sentence that expresses it, the system cannot update it reliably.

Contradictions must remain linked. A contradiction can be resolved, bounded,
refuted, residualized, or routed to review. It should not disappear because a
new paragraph reads better.

Revision history must not be overwritten. New evidence can change the current
state, but it cannot erase the fact that an older state existed, what supported
it, and why it changed.

Ledger identity must survive across editions. The AI/research book, Human
view, curated reader manuscript, diagrams, release notes, and future audio
scripts may express a claim differently. They should not create a new
unsupported claim merely by changing the surface.

Supersession must name what it replaces. A cleaner successor claim is not a
revision unless the ledger records the replaced claim, the reason for the
change, and the status of the old evidence.

## Failure Modes

The first failure is untracked assumption. The system relies on a statement
without deciding whether it is a claim, example, speculation, source-derived
fact, proof result, test result, or implementation report.

The second failure is belief inertia. A claim remains in circulation because
no artifact forces it to react when evidence expires, a contradiction appears,
a proof fails, a source is downgraded, or a reviewer objects.

The third failure is contradiction deletion. The prose gets revised, but the
old conflict vanishes instead of becoming a revision event.

The fourth failure is confidence laundering. The support state stays the same
while the language grows stronger across chapters, summaries, diagrams,
reader editions, or release artifacts.

The fifth failure is stale contradiction handling. A conflict is technically
recorded, but no owner, trigger, downgrade path, or review route keeps it
active. The architecture can then cite the confident version while the
contradiction sits inert in a ledger nobody reads.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a claim-record schema, a belief-revision
schema, and a small fixture suite.

The fixture suite should include a paragraph with several extracted claims, an
unsupported assumption recorded as a residual, a contradiction linked to the
affected claims, a downgrade event, a blocked promotion, and a human or
tribunal review route. That would not prove open-domain belief revision. It
would prove that the ledger can represent the state changes it is supposed to
govern.

The current repository has part of that minimum. Protocol validation checks
public record shape. `scripts/validate_claim_ledger_revision.py` checks
synthetic fixtures for revision-history preservation, contradiction routing,
support-state promotion blocking, surface propagation, residuals, and
non-claim language. `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` proves finite-record
properties: prior evidence and revision-history refs are preserved, an open
contradiction blocks promotion, and valid modeled belief-revision records keep
identity, support-state fields, revision reasons, history, and non-claim
boundaries.

The 2026-07-02 claim-ledger harness expansion adds semantic variant and
assumption-context checks. The harness now passes 5 valid fixture(s) and 7
expected-invalid fixture(s). It accepts a reader-facing variant only when scope
and support-state effect stay unchanged, splits a claim when a reader wording
drops a necessary source-boundary assumption, rejects broader wording laundered
as equivalent, rejects contested assumptions that are recorded but not routed,
and rejects variant merges whose surfaces are not synchronized. That gives the
reader-edition path a sharper ledger boundary without claiming a real semantic
equivalence engine.

That is useful, but narrow. The missing work is the hard part: extracting the
right claims from prose, detecting real contradictions, preserving semantic
equivalence across wording variants, checking citations, integrating proof or
review artifacts, and deciding whether a substantive belief should actually
change.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is epistemic version control for generated intelligence.

Every material claim would have durable identity. Every surface that repeats
the claim would point back to that identity. Every proof, citation, procedure,
benchmark, source note, review, failed replication, contradiction, downgrade,
split, merge, and retirement would become a ledger event. The system could
then answer questions that ordinary prose cannot answer well:

- Where does this claim appear?
- What evidence supports it?
- What evidence challenges it?
- Did its wording change its scope?
- Did a reader edition make it sound stronger?
- Which surfaces need repair after a downgrade?
- Which claims depend on a source, proof, benchmark, or reviewer that is now
  stale?
- Which contradictions are still open?

At that point, a self-improving system could change its mind without losing
its trail. It could regenerate a chapter, release note, diagram, audio script,
or implementation plan while checking whether the new surface stayed within
the ledger's support boundary. It could treat failed evidence as useful
information instead of reputational damage.

That endpoint is not implemented here. It requires claim extraction, semantic
matching, contradiction graphs, citation checks, proof and review integration,
surface synchronization, release gates, and negative controls strong enough to
catch confidence laundering.

## Evidence Boundary

The claim-ledger layer is evidence-aware, not evidence-promoted.

The live chapter remains at `argument` support. The local Lean predicates and
synthetic harnesses support only record discipline over declared finite
objects and fixtures. They do not show that the stack can read arbitrary prose,
find all material claims, detect contradictions correctly, adjudicate truth,
or keep every downstream surface synchronized in a deployed system.

The external belief-revision and truth-maintenance sources are lineage and
comparison points. They do not mean this repository has implemented AGM,
TMS, ATMS, open-domain truth maintenance, or automatic claim correction.

That boundary is part of the chapter's claim. A ledger that cannot say what it
has not proved is not a ledger; it is another prose surface with better field
names.

## Summary

Claim ledgers give belief an address. They keep claim identity, scope,
support state, provenance, contradiction links, uncertainty, surface refs,
revision reasons, and residuals visible as the book or system changes.

Belief revision is the maintenance operation that keeps that ledger honest. A
claim may be promoted, downgraded, split, merged, blocked, deprecated, or
retired, but the change must leave a history. The point is not to avoid being
wrong. The point is to make correction durable enough for later work to use.

## Handoff

Claim ledgers preserve belief identity. Some claims then need stronger
treatment than ordinary ledger records can provide.

The next layer is Spinoza Verification and Proof-Carrying Claims. It asks
which claims deserve proof-, citation-, procedure-, or justification-carrying
envelopes, what verifier result they require, what timeout or mismatch means,
and how the system refuses promotion when the envelope fails.
